reviews.author: Matthew Wilcox
reviews.summary: Reviewer Matthew Wilcox questioned the usefulness of the contended_release tracepoint, specifically asking for explanations on how it provides value in measuring hold time and capturing the holder's stack at release time.
reviews.raw_body:
As someone who's not an expert in this area (so please use short words
to explain it to me), why do we want to know how long this holder took
to release the lock from when it became contended?

I understand why we want to know how long any given waiter had to wait
to gain the lock (but we already have tracepoints which show that).

I also don't understand why we want to know the holder's stack at
release time.  The stack at contention-begin time will include
the point at which the lock was acquired which should be correlated
with where the lock was released.

Perhaps examples might help me understand why we want this?

reviews.author: Steven Rostedt
reviews.summary:
Steven Rostedt noted that the tracepoint placement for queued spinlocks and rwlocks should fire before the lock is released, not after, to accurately capture the holder's stack at release time.

The reviewer, Steven Rostedt, pointed out that the contended_release tracepoint would be useful for determining how long a critical section is held when a lock is highly contended by many tasks. He suggested that seeing the release time and location would help identify if the length of the critical section is causing the contention.
reviews.raw_body:
On Thu, 26 Mar 2026 15:55:21 +0000
Matthew Wilcox <willy@infradead.org> wrote:

---

Dmitry could give his own rationale for this, but I have my only use case.

This would be useful to find out how long the critical section is. If a
lock is highly contended by many tasks, you could get a high contention
time simply because other tasks are causing the delay for the waiter.
Seeing the release time and location would let you also know how long the
critical section was held, and if the length of the critical section is
causing the contention.

Having a stack trace of the release would differentiate the path that
released the lock, as there can be many places that release them. Although,
I have to admit, I'm not sure there are many different places locks are
released. Especially now that we have guard(), which will make all the
releases in a function at the same location.

-- Steve

reviews.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin (author)
reviews.summary:
The author is addressing Matthew Wilcox's suggestion to come up with generic instrumentation for contended release tracepoint, explaining that the current implementation provides a cause (holder time) and symptom (waiter time), but requires different treatments depending on the lock usage pattern.

The author addressed Matthew Wilcox's concern that the --lock-owner feature does not work for all lock types by explaining that contended_release tracepoint fires in the holder's context, providing the holder's stack at release time, which is necessary to identify the holders and what they were doing.
reviews.raw_body:
I think the simplest way to think about it is the following. Waiter time
is the symptom, while holder time is the cause.

The waiter-side contention_begin/contention_end tells us how long a
waiter waited, but that time can span multiple holders.

If a waiter waited 10 ms, we can not tell whether one holder held the
lock for 10 ms or five holders held it for 2 ms each. These need
different treatments: the first means shrink the critical section, the
second means reduce lock frequency or split the lock. Today we can not
distinguish between these cases from waiter-side data alone.

---

Holder's stack allows us to understand who exactly waiters were waiting
for to release the lock.

The stack at contention_begin time does not always include the holder's
stack. The --lock-owner feature works by reading the owner field from
the lock struct, but it only supports mutex and rwsem. For spinlocks,
queued rwlocks, semaphores, and several others, the waiter has no
visibility into the holder whatsoever.

contended_release fires in the holder's context, so we get the holder's
stack at release time. For spinlocks, this is the only way to get any
holder-side information.

Original motivation was zone lock contention (a spinlock) in Meta
production workloads. We could see waiters were blocked, but had no way
to identify the holders or what they were doing.

2026-03-26.patch_summary: This patch series adds a new tracepoint called contended_release that fires on the lock holder's side when a lock is released while there are waiters. This provides two main benefits: it allows for hold time estimation by correlating contention_end and contended_release events, and it captures the holder's stack at release time, which can be different from what is captured earlier if the holder does significant work between the waiter's arrival and unlock. The patch also fixes several issues in the previous versions, including spurious events, use-after-free errors, and build failures on certain architectures.

reviews.author: Usama Arif
reviews.summary: Reviewer Usama Arif suggested reordering the patches for better git bisectability and clarity in git blame, specifically moving refactoring work from patch 4 and 5 to earlier commits and adding all tracepoints in a single commit at the end.
reviews.raw_body:
Would it be better to reorder the patches? It would help with git
bisectability as well. Move the refractoring work in patch 4 and
5 (excluding adding the tracepoints ofcourse) earlier, and then add
all the tracepoints in the same commit at the end? It would help
in the future with git blame to see where all the tracepoints
were added as well.

reviews.author: Usama Arif
reviews.summary: Reviewer Usama Arif pointed out a compilation issue due to the variable 'waiter' being used outside its scope within an if block.
reviews.raw_body:
This won't compile? waiter is declared in the if block, so you are using
it outside scope here.

reviews.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin (author)
reviews.summary: Author addressed a concern about the scope of the 'waiter' variable by explaining that it is declared and assigned before the if block where the tracepoint is, thus still in scope.
reviews.raw_body:
Thanks for the feedback, Usama.

waiter is declared at function scope, right on top. It's also assigned
before the if block, so it's still in scope at the tracepoint.

reviews.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin (author)
reviews.summary: The author is addressing a suggestion from Usama Arif to reorder the patch series so that each refactoring commit is followed by the commit that uses it, making the motivation for the refactoring clear and easing review. The author prefers to keep the current ordering, citing that bisectability should be fine as-is since each commit compiles and works independently.
reviews.raw_body:
Thanks for the suggestion, Usama.

I'd prefer to keep the current ordering: each refactoring commit is
immediately followed by the commit that uses it. For example,
queued_spin_release() is factored out right before the commit that adds
the tracepoint to spinning locks. This makes the motivation for the
refactoring clear and should also ease the review since the context is
still fresh.

Bisectability should be fine as-is, each commit compiles and works
independently, since the refactoring patches do not introduce behavioral
changes on their own.

reviews.author: Usama Arif
reviews.summary: Reviewer Usama Arif noted that their review is based on an outdated version of the code, specifically mentioning that the waiter variable is declared differently in the current mm-new branch.
reviews.raw_body:
Ah ok, I was reviewing on top of mm-new branch from today where waiter
is declared in the if block. Probably something changed related to
locking/tracing and its not in mm-new yet.

2026-03-31.patch_summary: This patch series adds a new tracepoint called contended_release that fires on the lock holder's side when a lock is released while there are waiters. This provides two main benefits: it allows for hold time estimation by correlating contention_end and contended_release events, and it captures the holder's stack at release time, which can be different from what is captured earlier if the holder does significant work between the waiter's arrival and unlock. The patch also fixes several issues in the previous versions, including spurious events, use-after-free errors, and build failures on certain architectures.
